title: How to Fix Scratches on Glasses: What Works, What Doesn't, and When to Replace
description: Can you fix scratched glasses at home? Honest advice on DIY scratch removal, what actually works, when it's time for new lenses, and how to prevent scratches.
tldr: You can't truly fix scratches on modern coated lenses — most DIY remedies make things worse by stripping anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings. Minor surface scuffs can sometimes be buffed on uncoated lenses, but deep scratches mean it's time for replacement lenses.

tldrStats.label: DIY methods that fix coated lenses safely
Cause of lens scratches: improper cleaning
Typical lens replacement cost in Canada

definition.text: Glasses lens scratches are surface damage to eyeglass lenses caused by abrasion, impact, or improper cleaning. Scratches can range from minor surface scuffs that don't affect vision to deep gouges that distort light and impair visual clarity. Most modern lenses have anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings that complicate DIY repair attempts.

sections.h2: The Honest Truth About Scratched Glasses
sections.content:
Search the internet for "how to fix scratched glasses" and you'll find dozens of suggestions: toothpaste, baking soda, car wax, furniture polish, glass etching cream. Most of these tips circulate endlessly on social media without anyone mentioning the catch.

Here's the catch: modern prescription lenses aren't bare glass or plastic. They're coated with multiple layers — anti-reflective coating, scratch-resistant coating, UV coating, sometimes blue light filtering or hydrophobic coatings. These coatings are measured in microns. Any abrasive substance that's strong enough to buff out a scratch will strip these coatings off entirely, leaving you with hazier, more glare-prone lenses than you started with.

So let's be realistic about what's fixable and what isn't.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: What Doesn't Work (Despite What the Internet Says)
sections.content:
Toothpaste: The most commonly recommended DIY fix. Toothpaste is a mild abrasive that can buff minor scratches on uncoated plastic — but it will strip anti-reflective coatings immediately, creating a cloudy, uneven surface. Worse than the scratch.

Baking soda paste: Same problem as toothpaste. The abrasive particles remove coatings and can create micro-scratches across the entire lens surface.

Car wax or furniture polish: These can temporarily fill very superficial scratches, making them less visible. But the effect is temporary, and the wax buildup can attract dust and create its own optical distortion.

Glass etching cream: Some guides suggest using armour etch to remove the damaged coating layer entirely. This does remove the coating — along with all its benefits. You're left with bare plastic that scratches even more easily and reflects more glare. This is a one-way trip to worse lenses.

For uncoated plastic lenses (increasingly rare, but some basic readers and safety glasses have them), a gentle buff with a microfiber cloth and a tiny amount of non-gel toothpaste can reduce the visibility of very light surface scuffs. This won't fix deep scratches, and it only works because there's no coating to damage.

For minor coating damage (not deep scratches), a lens-specific scratch filler product — sold at some optical stores — can temporarily reduce the visibility of light marks. These are essentially clear-filling compounds that sit in the scratch and reduce light scatter. They're not permanent and need reapplication.

For anything deeper than a surface scuff, replacement is the only real fix.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Replace your lenses when scratches:

  • Are visible in your line of sight and cause light scatter or halos
  • Make you squint or tilt your head to see around them
  • Are accompanied by coating peeling, crazing (tiny cracks in the coating), or cloudiness
  • Affect your driving vision, especially at night when scratches cause glare from oncoming headlights

You don't always need new frames — many optical stores can replace just the lenses in your existing frames, which saves money if the frames are still in good shape.

At Charm Optical in South Edmonton, the team can assess whether your scratches warrant lens replacement and give you a quote on the spot. If your prescription is current, same-day lens replacement may be available for single vision lenses.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

sections.h2: How Much Does Lens Replacement Cost?
sections.content:
In Canada, replacing lenses in your existing frames typically costs:

  • Basic single vision: $100-200
  • Single vision with premium coatings: $150-300
  • Progressive lenses: $300-600+
  • Specialty lenses (high-index, photochromic): $250-500+

If you have insurance coverage, much of this may be covered under your optical benefit. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and other major providers typically cover lens replacements within your benefit period. Check whether you have unused optical benefits before paying out of pocket.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

Insurance makes a significant difference. Most employer health plans in Alberta cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurance providers — Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and more — so you only pay the difference at the counter. No paperwork, no reimbursement wait. Book an eye exam to get your prescription checked and learn exactly what your plan covers before you shop.

sections.h2: How to Prevent Scratches in the First Place
sections.content:
Prevention is genuinely the best strategy here. Most scratches are caused by the same few habits:

  • Cleaning with your shirt, paper towel, or tissue: These fabrics contain fibres that scratch coatings. Use only a clean microfiber cloth.
  • Setting glasses face-down: The lenses touch whatever surface you set them on. Always fold them and rest them on their temples, or use a case.
  • Tossing them in a bag or pocket loose: Keys, coins, and pens are lens killers. Use a hard case.
  • Dry-wiping dirty lenses: Rubbing dust and grit across a dry lens is like sanding it. Rinse with water first, then wipe with microfiber.

The single best habit: rinse your lenses under lukewarm running water before wiping them. This washes away dust particles that would otherwise scratch the surface when you wipe.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: Scratch-Resistant Coatings: Are They Worth It?
sections.content:
Yes. Scratch-resistant coatings don't make lenses scratch-proof, but they significantly increase surface hardness. A coated lens can handle minor daily abrasion that would visibly scratch an uncoated lens.

Most quality prescription lenses now include a scratch-resistant coating as standard or a low-cost add-on. Some manufacturers offer scratch warranties — if the coating fails within a certain period, they'll replace the lenses. Ask about warranty coverage when ordering lenses.

Your optician at Charm Optical can explain which coatings come standard with each lens type and what upgrades are available. If you're hard on your glasses, investing in a premium coating package pays for itself.

Pricing varies significantly depending on where you buy and what lens features you choose. In Canada, the total cost of a complete pair of glasses (frame + lenses) typically breaks down as follows: budget options start at $150-$250 for basic frames with standard single vision lenses. Mid-range options with anti-reflective coating and a name-brand frame run $300-$500. Premium designer frames with progressive or high-index lenses can reach $600-$900+. The biggest variable is usually the lens — a basic single vision lens costs far less than a digital freeform progressive with premium coatings.

Insurance makes a significant difference. Most employer health plans in Alberta cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurance providers — Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, Desjardins, and more — so you only pay the difference at the counter. No paperwork, no reimbursement wait. Book an eye exam to get your prescription checked and learn exactly what your plan covers before you shop.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: Does toothpaste remove scratches from glasses?
faqs.a: On uncoated plastic lenses, toothpaste may reduce minor surface scuffs. On modern coated lenses (which is most prescription eyewear), toothpaste strips the anti-reflective and scratch-resistant coatings, making the lenses worse. It's not recommended for prescription glasses.

faqs.q: Can an optician fix scratched lenses?
faqs.a: Opticians can't repair scratches on coated lenses. What they can do is replace the lenses in your existing frames, often at a lower cost than buying a complete new pair. Charm Optical in South Edmonton offers lens replacement with same-day service for many single vision prescriptions.

faqs.q: How do I clean glasses without scratching them?
faqs.a: Rinse lenses under lukewarm water first to remove dust particles. Apply a drop of dish soap (no lotions or moisturizers) and gently rub with your fingers. Rinse again and dry with a clean microfiber cloth. Never dry-wipe dirty lenses.

faqs.q: Are scratched lenses bad for your eyes?
faqs.a: Scratches don't directly damage your eyes, but they can cause glare, light scatter, and reduced clarity that leads to eye strain and headaches. Deep scratches in your line of sight can also be a safety hazard while driving, especially at night.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover lens replacement for scratched glasses?
faqs.a: Most Alberta insurance plans cover lens replacement within your benefit period, regardless of why you need new lenses. Check your remaining optical benefit balance — you may have coverage you haven't used. Charm Optical offers direct billing to most major Alberta insurance providers.
